title: The World-Building Doors Are Open, Again. | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: Josh Elman, joining a16z as a consumer partner, argues that AI plus a generation of native 'world-builder' users (gen-alpha raised on Roblox/Minecraft) has reopened a wave of greenfield opportunity in AI-first consumer apps after years of a stagnant, fully-built-out consumer landscape.

insights.claim: After a multi-year period where consumer tech seemed 'dead' because the worlds users wanted were already built out (Instagram, the iPhone), AI and a new user cohort have reopened large-scale greenfield opportunity in AI-first consumer apps.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Signals a major VC reallocating toward early-stage AI consumer startups; watch a16z consumer deal flow as a leading indicator of where capital chases the next platform shift.
insights.quote: In recent years, there was a period where it seemed like consumer was pretty dead. And that was really just a way of saying, “the worlds that consumers wanted to inhabit were all pretty built out”.
insights.locator: In recent years, there was a period

insights.claim: Billions of people will soon discover new AI-native ways to explore, get things done, learn, shop, manage money, travel, and communicate, with these consumer activities being reinvented around new interfaces, mirroring prior dot-com, social, and mobile waves.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Implies broad re-platforming of incumbent consumer verticals (commerce, fintech, travel, learning); incumbents face disruption risk while AI-native challengers gain entry points.
insights.locator: So what does this all mean for consumer founders

themes.theme: Drivers: AI capability and a world-builder generation

insights.claim: ChatGPT is the biggest consumer AI breakout to date, having introduced open-ended conversations and relationships with software, and that growth opportunity is still at its very beginning.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Frames conversational interfaces as early-stage rather than saturated; supports continued investment thesis in chat-native consumer products.
insights.locator: The biggest consumer breakout so far, ChatGPT

insights.claim: Gen-alpha consumers raised on Roblox and Minecraft are native world-builders who expect to customize or build any app for themselves and their friends, creating demand for highly malleable, user-creatable consumer software.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Suggests product opportunities in user-generated and customizable consumer apps; favor platforms that let end-users build rather than just consume.
insights.quote: They came up playing Roblox and Minecraft, they have no preconceived limitations about what an app is, or what they can do with it.
insights.locator: we have a new wave of users

insights.claim: Falling inference costs from open and on-device models are making AI-first consumer products economically viable at scale.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Cheaper inference lowers the unit-economics barrier for consumer AI apps; watch open-weight and on-device model adoption as enablers of consumer margin viability.

insights.claim: The differentiating value in AI is not the models themselves but the harnesses, loops, and context built around them, which is where new product opportunities will emerge.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Implies durable consumer value accrues to application/orchestration layers rather than to raw model providers; favor startups owning the harness and context layer.
insights.quote: it isn’t the models that matter, but the harnesses, loops, and context which will lead to so many new opportunities ahead.

insights.claim: Most great consumer products start out looking like a dismissable toy, as seen with Twitter, Robinhood, and Musical.ly/TikTok, so apparent triviality at launch is a feature rather than a disqualifier for durable platforms.
insights.speaker: Josh Elman
insights.so_what: Provides a screening heuristic: don't dismiss early consumer products that look frivolous; the next durable platform may currently be derided as a toy.
insights.quote: most great products start out looking like a toy
insights.locator: It can be tough; it can look silly
